questions.question: An aeroplane is flying due West, and it experiences a resistive force in the Eastern direction. What is this force known as?
questions.explanation: Drag is the resistive force that opposes an object's motion through a fluid (air or water), acting opposite to the direction of motion.

questions.question: A heavy trunk is sitting on a wooden floor. You push it with a small force, but it does not budge. Which force is exactly balancing your push?
questions.explanation: Static friction matches the applied force to keep the object stationary, up to its maximum value. If the trunk doesn't move, static friction equals your push.

questions.question: Why is it generally more difficult to start a heavy box moving than it is to keep it moving at a constant speed?
options: Air resistance is lower when the box is stationary
The coefficient of static friction is greater than the coefficient of kinetic friction
Inertia only exists when an object is at rest
The mass of the box decreases slightly once it starts moving
questions.explanation: The coefficient of static friction (μ_s) is typically greater than kinetic friction (μ_k), so the maximum static friction force exceeds the kinetic friction force.

questions.question: If a spacecraft is drifting in deep space (far from any planets) at a constant velocity, what is the net force acting on it?
options: A force equal to its mass
questions.explanation: In deep space with negligible gravity and no friction, constant velocity means zero net force (Newton's First Law).

questions.question: Which of the following would successfully reduce the friction between a sliding metal block and a metal table?
options: Roughing the surface of the table with sandpaper
Adding a layer of oil between the surfaces
Increasing the weight of the metal block
Increasing the surface area of the block in contact
questions.explanation: Lubricants like oil fill surface irregularities and reduce the coefficient of friction between the two surfaces.

questions.question: When a person walks forward on a sidewalk, in which direction does the friction force from the ground act on their shoes?
questions.explanation: The shoes push backward on the ground, so the ground pushes forward on the shoes via static friction, propelling the person forward.

questions.question: What happens to the friction force acting on a car's tires if the driver slams on the brakes and the tires start skidding?
options: The friction changes from static to kinetic
The friction changes from kinetic to static
questions.explanation: When tires skid, they slide across the road surface, so static friction is replaced by the weaker kinetic friction.

questions.question: An object is moving with constant speed in a straight line. If the applied force is exactly equal to the friction force, the object will:
options: Continue moving at the same constant speed
questions.explanation: When applied force equals friction, net force is zero, so the object continues at constant velocity (Newton's First Law).

questions.question: Which of the following factors does NOT affect the magnitude of the kinetic friction force between two solid objects?
options: The speed at which the objects are sliding
The type of materials in contact
questions.explanation: Kinetic friction (f_k = μ_k × F_N) depends on the coefficient of friction and normal force, not sliding speed. Roughness and material type affect μ_k.

questions.question: Which of the following is associated with the propulsive force of an airplane's engine?
questions.explanation: Thrust is the forward propulsive force produced by an airplane's engines, overcoming drag to move the aircraft forward.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered friction, drag, and applied forces!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review static vs. kinetic friction and the four forces of flight.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on when static friction applies vs. kinetic friction.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: types of friction, drag, and Newton's First Law of motion.
